title: Approximating with powers of 10
description: Practice comparing two real-world quantities by approximating them with powers of 10.

assessment_items.question_content:
A worker bee has a mass of $0.00011 \text{ kg}$.

**Choose the best approximation of the mass of a worker bee.**
[[☃ radio 1]]

An African elephant has a mass of $8139 \text{ kg}$.

**Choose the best approximation of the mass of an African elephant.**
[[☃ radio 2]]

**Approximately how many times as large is the mass of an African elephant as the mass of a worker bee?**
[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
First, let's approximate the mass of a worker bee.

$\phantom{\approx }0.00011\text{ kg}$

$\approx 0.0001\text{ kg}$

$\approx1 \cdot 10^{-4}\text{ kg}$

Next, let's approximate the mass of an African elephant.

$\phantom{\approx }8139\text{ kg}$

$\approx 8000\text{ kg}$

$\approx8 \cdot 10^{3}\text{ kg}$

Now, let's divide to approximate how many times greater the mass of an African elephant is than the mass of a worker bee.

$\phantom{=} \dfrac{8\cdot10^{3}}{1\cdot10^{-4}}$

$=\dfrac{8}{1} \cdot \dfrac{10^{3}}{10^{-4}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Peggy the Pig built a house of sticks. The stick-house can withstand wind speeds up to $47 \text{ km}$ per hour.

**Choose the best approximation of the highest wind speed that Peggy's stick-house can withstand.**
[[☃ radio 1]]

Patricia the Pig built a very strong house of steel. The steel-house can withstand wind speeds up to $4928 \text{ km}$ per hour.

**Choose the best approximation of the highest wind speed that Patricia's steel-house can withstand.**
[[☃ radio 2]]

**In terms of ability to withstand wind speed, approximately how many times as strong is Patricia's steel-house  as Peggy's stick-house?**
[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
First, let's approximate the highest wind speed that Peggy's stick-house can withstand.

$\phantom{\approx }47\text{ km}$ per hour

$\approx 50\text{ km}$ per hour

$\approx5 \cdot 10^{1}\text{ km}$ per hour

Next, let's approximate the highest wind speed that Patricia's steel-house can withstand.

$\phantom{=}4928\text{ km}$ per hour

$\approx 5000\text{ km}$ per hour

$\approx5 \cdot 10^{3}\text{ km}$ per hour

Now, let's divide to approximate how many times stronger Patricia's steel-house  is than Peggy's stick-house.

$\phantom{=} \dfrac{5\cdot10^{3}}{5\cdot10^{1}}$

$=\dfrac{5}{5} \cdot \dfrac{10^{3}}{10^{1}}$

$=1 \cdot 10^2$

assessment_items.question_content:
An average human stride is  $1.44 \text{ m}$ long.

**Choose the best approximation of the length of a human stride.**
[[☃ radio 1]]

A marathon is $42{,}195 \text{ m}$ long.

**Choose the best approximation of the length of a marathon.**
[[☃ radio 2]]

**Approximately how many strides does it take to complete a marathon?**
[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
First, let's approximate the length of a human stride.

$\phantom{\approx }1.44\text{ m}$

$\approx1\text{ m}$

$\approx1 \cdot 10^{0}\text{ m}$

Next, let's approximate the length of a marathon.

$\phantom{=}42{,}195\text{ m}$

$\approx 40{,}000\text{ m}$

$\approx4 \cdot 10^{4}\text{ m}$

Now, let's divide to approximate how many strides it takes to complete a marathon.

$\phantom{=} \dfrac{4\cdot10^{4}}{1\cdot10^{0}}$ strides

$=\dfrac{4}{1} \cdot \dfrac{10^{4}}{10^{0}}$ strides
$=4 \cdot 10^4$ strides

assessment_items.question_content:
Liam's mass is $102.1 \text{ kg}$.

**Choose the best approximation of Liam's mass.**
[[☃ radio 1]]

Earth has an estimated mass of $5{,}972{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000 \text{ kg}$.

**Choose the best approximation of the Earth's mass.**
[[☃ radio 2]]

**Approximately how many times as large is the Earth's mass as Liam's mass?**
[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
First, let's approximate Liam's mass.

$\phantom{\approx}102.1\text{ kg}$

$\approx 100\text{ kg}$

$\approx1 \cdot 10^2\text{ kg}$

Now, let's divide to approximate how many times greater the Earth's mass is than Liam's mass.

$\phantom{=} \dfrac{6\cdot10^{24}}{1\cdot10^{2}}$

$=\dfrac{6}{1} \cdot \dfrac{10^{24}}{10^2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The Mississippi River flows at a rate of $16{,}790$ cubic meters per second.

**Choose the best approximation of the flow rate of the Mississippi River.**
[[☃ radio 1]]

The Gulf of Mexico has an estimated volume of $2{,}434{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000$ cubic meters.

**Choose the best approximation of the volume of the Gulf of Mexico.**
[[☃ radio 2]]

**Approximately how many seconds would it take for the Mississippi River to fill the volume of the Gulf of Mexico?**
[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
First, let's approximate the flow rate of the Mississippi River.

$\phantom{\approx}16{,}790$ cubic meters per second

$\approx 20{,}000$ cubic meters per second

$\approx2 \cdot 10^4$ cubic meters per second

Next, let's approximate the volume of the Gulf of Mexico.

$\phantom{\approx}2{,}434{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000$ cubic meters

$\approx2{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000$ cubic meters

$\approx2\cdot10^{15}$ cubic meters

Now, let's divide to approximate how many seconds it would take for the Mississippi River to fill the volume of the Gulf of Mexico.

$\phantom{=} \dfrac{2\cdot10^{15}}{2\cdot10^{4}}$ seconds

$=\dfrac{2}{2} \cdot \dfrac{10^{15}}{10^4}$ seconds
$= 1 \cdot 10^{11}$ seconds

assessment_items.question_content:
In $2014$, the population of the United States was about $318{,}900{,}000$ people.

**Choose the best approximation of the United States population in $2014$.**
[[☃ radio 1]]

In $2014$, the population of the world was about $7{,}125{,}000{,}000$ people.

**Choose the best approximation of the world population in $2014$.**
[[☃ radio 2]]

**Approximately how many times as large was the world population as the United States population in $2014$?**
[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
First, let's approximate the population of the United States in $2014$.

$\phantom{\approx }318{,}900{,}000$ people

$\approx 300{,}000{,}000$ people

$\approx3 \cdot 10^{8}$ people

Next, let's approximate the population of the world in $2014$.

$\phantom{=}7{,}125{,}000{,}000$ people

$\approx 7{,}000{,}000{,}000$ people

$\approx7 \cdot 10^{9}$ people

Now, let's divide to approximate how many times larger the world population was than the United States population in $2014$.

$\phantom{=} \dfrac{7\cdot10^{9}}{3\cdot10^{8}}$

$=\dfrac{7}{3} \cdot \dfrac{10^{9}}{10^{8}}$

$\approx2 \cdot 10^1$
$\approx 20$

assessment_items.question_content:
There are about $31{,}500{,}000$ seconds in a year.  


**Choose the best approximation of the number of seconds in a year.**
[[☃ radio 1]]

Light travels at a rate of about $9{,}460{,}528{,}400{,}000{,}000 \text{ m}$ per year.

**Choose the best approximation of how many meters light travels per year.**
[[☃ radio 2]]

**Approximately how many meters does light travel per second?**
[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
First, let's approximate the number of seconds in a year.

$\phantom{\approx }31{,}500{,}000$ seconds

$\approx 32{,}000{,}000$ seconds

$\approx3 \cdot 10^7$ seconds

Next, let's approximate the number of meters light travels in a year.

$\phantom{\approx}9{,}460{,}528{,}400{,}000{,}000$ meters

$\approx9{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000$ meters

$\approx9\cdot10^{15}$ meters

Now, let's divide to approximate how many meters light travels per second.

$\phantom{=} \dfrac{9\cdot10^{15}}{3\cdot10^{7}}$ meters per second

$=\dfrac{9}{3} \cdot \dfrac{10^{15}}{10^7}$ meters per second
$\approx 3 \cdot 10^8$ meters per second

assessment_items.question_content:
Each page of a document requires $8113 \text{ B}$ (bytes). 

**Choose the best approximation of the number of bytes required for a page of a document.**
[[☃ radio 1]]

Rhonda has a USB drive that can hold $8{,}000{,}000{,}000\text{ B}$.

**Choose the best approximation of the number of bytes that Rhonda's USB drive can hold.**
[[☃ radio 2]]

**Approximately how many pages can Rhonda's USB drive hold?**
[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
First, let's approximate the number of bytes required for a page of a document.

$\phantom{\approx }8113\text{ B}$

$\approx 8000\text{ B}$

$\approx8 \cdot 10^{3}\text{ B}$

Next, let's approximate the number of bytes that Rhonda's USB drive can hold.

$\phantom{=}8{,}000{,}000{,}000\text{ B}$

$=8 \cdot 10^{9}\text{ B}$

Now, let's divide to approximate how many pages Rhonda's USB drive can hold.

$\phantom{=} \dfrac{8\cdot10^{9}}{8\cdot10^{3}}$ pages

$=\dfrac{8}{8} \cdot \dfrac{10^{9}}{10^{3}}$ pages
$=1\cdot 10 ^{6}$ pages

assessment_items.question_content:
Approximately $1{,}000{,}000$ people cross the Shibuya Crossing intersection each day.

**Choose the best approximation of the number of people that cross Shibuya Crossing each day.**
[[☃ radio 1]]

There are $1440$ minutes in each day.

**Choose the best approximation of the number of minutes in each day.**
[[☃ radio 2]]

**Approximately how many people cross Shibuya Crossing each minute?**
[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
First, let's approximate the number of people that cross Shibuya Crossing each day.

$\phantom{=}1{,}000{,}000$ people


$=1 \cdot 10^{6}$ people

Next, let's approximate the number of minutes in each day.

$\phantom{\approx }1440$ minutes

$\approx 1000$ minutes

$\approx1 \cdot 10^{3}$ minutes

Now, let's divide to approximate how many people cross Shibuya Crossing each minute.

$\phantom{=} \dfrac{1\cdot10^{6}}{1\cdot10^{3}}$ people per minute

$=\dfrac{1}{1} \cdot \dfrac{10^{6}}{10^{3}}$ people per minute
$=1 \cdot 10 ^{3}$ people per minute

assessment_items.question_content:
The diameter of a human red blood cell is $0.000009 \text{ m}$.
The diameter of a cold virus is $0.00000003 \text{ m}$. 

**Approximately how many times as large is the diameter of a red blood cell as the diameter of a cold virus?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's estimate the diameter of a red blood cell.

$\phantom{=}0.000009 \text{ m}$



$=9 \cdot 10^{-6}\text{ m}$ 
Next, let's estimate the diameter of a cold virus.


$\phantom{\approx}0.00000003 \text{ m}$



$=3 \cdot 10^{-8}\text{ m}$
Now, let's divide to approximate how many times greater the diameter of a red blood cell is than the diameter of a cold virus.

$\phantom{=}\dfrac{9\cdot10^{-6}}{3\cdot10^{-8}}$

$=3\cdot\dfrac{10^{-6}}{10^{-8}}$

$=3 \cdot10^2$  

$3\cdot10^2$

assessment_items.question_content:
Harvey Porter is a wizard.  He waved his wand and made a grain of sand that had a diameter of $0.00002 \text{ m}$  grow to a diameter $0.006 \text{ m}$.  

**Approximately how many times as large is the diameter of the grain of sand now as it was before Harvey Porter waved his wand?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's estimate the new diameter of the grain of sand.

$\phantom{=}0.006 \text{ m}$



$=6 \cdot 10^{-3}\text{ m}$

Next, let's estimate the original diameter of the grain of sand.


$\phantom{=}0.00002 \text{ m}$



$=2 \cdot 10^{-5} \text{ m}$

Now, let's divide to find out how many times greater the diameter of the grain of sand is now than it was before Harvey Porter waved his wand.

$\phantom{=}\dfrac{6\cdot10^{-3}}{2\cdot10^{-5}}$

$=\dfrac{6}{2}\cdot\dfrac{10^{-3}}{10^{-5}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Charlotte's annual salary is $29{,}354$ dollars. She is hoping to win the World Lottery which has a grand prize of $6{,}156{,}782{,}221$ dollars. 

**Approximately how many times as large is the World Lottery's grand prize as Charlotte's annual salary?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's estimate the World Lottery's grand prize.

$\phantom{\approx}6{,}156{,}782{,}221$ dollars

$\approx6{,}000{,}000{,}000$ dollars

$\approx6 \cdot 10^{9}$ dollars
Next, let's estimate Charlotte's salary.


$\phantom{\approx}29{,}354$ dollars

$\approx 30{,}000$ dollars

$\approx3 \cdot 10^{4}$ dollars
Now, let's divide to approximate how many times larger the World Lottery's grand prize is than Charlotte's annual salary.

$\phantom{=}\dfrac{6\cdot10^9}{3\cdot10^4}$

$=\dfrac{6}{3}\cdot\dfrac{10^9}{10^4}$

$=2\cdot10^5$

assessment_items.question_content:
Without any equipment, you can see stars that are $2{,}800{,}000$ light-years away.  By looking through a small telescope, you can see stars that are $3{,}112{,}000{,}000$ light-years away.


**Approximately how many times as far can you see using a small telescope as without any equipment?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's estimate how far you can see without any equipment.

$\phantom{\approx}2{,}800{,}000$ light-years

$\approx 3{,}000{,}000$ light-years

$\approx3 \cdot 10^{6}$ light-years

Next, let's estimate how far you can see using a small telescope.

$\phantom{\approx}3{,}112{,}000{,}000$ light-years

$\approx3{,}000{,}000{,}000$ light-years

$\approx3 \cdot 10^{9}$ light-years

Now, let's divide to approximate how many times farther you can see using a small telescope than without any equipment.

$\phantom{=}\dfrac{3\cdot10^9}{3\cdot10^6}$

$=\dfrac33\cdot\dfrac{10^9}{10^6}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Santa and his elves had a workshop that allowed them to  produce $22{,}912{,}546{,}992$ toys each year. 

The world population increased, so they built a new workshop. The new workshop allows them to produce $4{,}134{,}232{,}638{,}937$ toys each year.


**Approximately how many times as many toys can the new workshop produce each year compared to the old workshop?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's estimate how many toys the new workshop can produce each year.

$\phantom{\approx}4{,}134{,}232{,}638{,}937$ toys

$\approx 4{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000$ toys

$\approx4 \cdot 10^{12}$ toys

Next, let's estimate how many toys the old workshop can produce each year.

$\phantom{\approx}22{,}912{,}546{,}992$ toys

$\approx 20{,}000{,}000{,}000$ toys

$\approx2 \cdot 10^{10}$ toys

Now, let's divide to approximate how many times more toys the new workshop can produce each year compared to the old workshop.  

$\phantom{=}\dfrac{4\cdot10^{12}}{2\cdot10^{10}}$

$=\dfrac{4}{2}\cdot\dfrac{10^{12}}{10^{10}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
James had a peach that was $98 \text{ mm}$ in diameter. One day he watered it with a magical solution, and it grew to  $188{,}869 \text{ mm}$ in diameter.

**Approximately how many times as large did the diameter of the peach become after James watered it?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's estimate the diameter of the peach after James watered it.

$\phantom{\approx}188{,}869 \text{ mm}$

$\approx 200{,}000 \text{ mm}$

$\approx2 \cdot 10^{5} \text{ mm}$

Next, let's estimate the diameter of the peach before James watered it.

$\phantom{\approx}98 \text{ mm}$

$\approx 100 \text{ mm}$

$\approx1 \cdot 10^{2} \text{ mm}$

Now, let's divide to approximate how many times larger the diameter of the peach became after James watered it.


$\phantom{=}\dfrac{2\cdot10^5}{1\cdot10^2}$

$=\dfrac{2}1\cdot\dfrac{10^5}{10^2}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Ten years ago, Rapunzel's hair was $19 \text{ cm}$ long.  Now her hair is $20{,}193 \text{ cm}$ long.

**Approximately how many times as long is Rapunzel's hair now as it was ten years ago?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's estimate Rapunzel's hair length now.

$\phantom{\approx}20{,}193 \text{ cm}$

$\approx 20{,}000 \text{ cm}$

$\approx2 \cdot 10^{4} \text{ cm}$

Next, let's estimate Rapunzel's hair length ten years ago.

$\phantom{\approx}19 \text{ cm}$

$\approx 20 \text{ cm}$

$\approx2 \cdot 10^{1} \text{ cm}$

Now, let's divide to approximate how many times longer Rapunzel's hair is now than it was ten years ago.

$\phantom{=}\dfrac{2\cdot10^4}{2\cdot10^1}$

$=1\cdot\dfrac{10^4}{10^1}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The table shown below gives the approximate $2014$ earnings for various athletes.


**Approximately how many times as large were Ronald Christian's $2014$ earnings as Forest Thorton's?**

Athlete | $2014$ earnings (in dollars) 
:-: | :-:  
Ronald Christian | $92{,}000{,}000$ 
James Leon | $20{,}300{,}000$ 
Mason Payton | $15{,}700{,}000$ 
Ryan Nader| $14{,}800{,}000$ 
Forest Thorton| $30{,}000$ 


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's estimate Ronald Christian's $2014$ earnings.

$\phantom{\approx}92{,}000{,}000$ dollars

$\approx 90{,}000{,}000$ dollars

$\approx 9 \cdot 10^7$ dollars

Next, let's estimate Forest Thorton's $2014$ earnings.

$\phantom{\approx}30{,}000$ dollars

$= 3 \cdot 10^4$ dollars

Now, let's divide to approximate how many times greater Ronald Christian's $2014$ earnings were than Forest Thorton's.

$\phantom{=}\dfrac{9\cdot10^7}{3\cdot10^4}$

$=\dfrac93\cdot\dfrac{10^7}{10^4}$
$\approx 3 \cdot10^3$

assessment_items.question_content:
The table shown below gives the estimated number of text messages sent in the U.S. by year.

**Approximately how many times as many text messages were sent in the U.S. in $2011$ as in $2005$?**

Year | Text messages sent in U.S. 
:-: | :-:  
$2005$ | $118{,}000{,}000{,}000$ 
$2007$| $577{,}000{,}000{,}000$ 
$2009$ | $1{,}198{,}000{,}000{,}000$ 
$2011$ | $2{,}357{,}000{,}000{,}000$  



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's estimate the number of text messages sent in the U.S. in $2011$.

$\phantom{\approx}2{,}357{,}000{,}000{,}000$ text messages

$\approx 2{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000$ text messages

$\approx 2 \cdot 10^{12}$ text messages

First, let's estimate the number of text messages sent in the U.S. in $2005$.

$\phantom{\approx}118{,}000{,}000{,}000$ text messages

$\approx 100{,}000{,}000{,}000$ text messages

$\approx 1 \cdot 10^{11}$ text messages

Now, let's divide to approximate how many times more text messages were sent in the U.S. in $2011$ than in $2005$. 

$\phantom{=}\dfrac{2\cdot10^{12}}{1\cdot10^{11}}$

$=\dfrac{2}{1}\cdot\dfrac{10^{12}}{10^{11}}$
